In Greek tradition, the *syrtos* or *hora* carries this meaning; in Kurdish *govend*, the chain of hands binds the community together in spirit. In the Balkans, the circle was thought to guard against evil spirits slipping between the dancers.

When such an ending appears in today's cultural exchange events, it is not so much about a perfect performance, but about weaving a human rope — each person a knot — to remind everyone that however different the languages and costumes, each heartbeat can still join the same measure.

Even for someone standing apart and watching from the edge, the meaning can be felt: you are looking at a moving emblem of unity, where the hands serve as bridges and the steps as promises. That is why it leaves such a warm aftertaste in memory — because the circle, once closed, never truly breaks, it just waits for the next time.

conversations.content: Tell me, why is it that in some of those old, hand-drawn adventure books the shadows and clothing folds look almost like the engravings from 19th-century history volumes? Was it just fashion, or was someone trying to say something?

Ah, that is a very good and perceptive question — one that reveals you’ve been looking at the pages not only for their story, but for their craft.

What you’ve noticed, that sharp contrast work in the shadows and the minute, parallel hatch lines in the folds of cloaks and uniforms, often springs from the roots of early comic and graphic novel art in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Many artists of that period were either trained in, or heavily influenced by, printmaking techniques like wood engraving, copperplate etching, and steel engraving — the very methods used in illustrated history books, encyclopedias, and serialized magazines of the Victorian era.

Before offset printing and modern color processes became affordable, illustrators worked within the constraints of monochrome reproduction. Fine line hatching was not only an aesthetic choice, but a necessary one: it allowed for texture, depth, and tonal variation with just black ink on white paper. When comics began to evolve as a storytelling medium in the 1930s and beyond, a lot of those early artists carried over — consciously or unconsciously — the visual vocabulary of the engravings they admired, or even directly studied from.

There’s also a symbolic undercurrent. By evoking the look of historical engravings, these works could lend their fictional worlds an air of authenticity, as if saying to the reader: *this is worthy of record, as much as any real battle or era.* In the case of adventure stories, especially those set in colonial frontiers or imagined Eastern lands, this stylistic choice gave a reassuring familiarity to Western audiences who were used to seeing their history presented in that manner.

The rain itself has not changed — but we have. Our past selves heard it through one window, our present selves through another.

There is also the truth that sounds carry the seasons of our own story. If a certain chapter of life was heavy and it rained much during those days, the ear may keep the association long after those clouds have gone. It’s a little like how an old song once danced to at a wedding can bring tears years later, if the people from that dance are gone.

Some elders say this is a mercy — the soul telling us gently to listen inward more than outward in certain hours. Others might say it is simply the passing of taste, like a favorite sherbet in youth that now feels too sweet on the tongue.

So perhaps, Mehmet Bey, the sound of the rain is still a lullaby… just being sung to a different man than the young fellow who once fell asleep to it.

knowledge_query.preference: Takes photographs of people

conversations.content: Why is it that in old village photographs, the faces seem so solemn, almost as if they carry the whole weight of the times upon them?

Holding a smile for that long could turn it brittle, so most chose a neutral, steady expression. But there is more to it than the shutters and silver plates.

Back then, you see, a photograph was not a casual thing. In many villages, a picture might be taken only once in a lifetime, perhaps when someone was leaving for military service, or just before a wedding, or to send to a relative far away who might never come home. These moments carried the weight of farewell, of duty, of hope in uncertain times. To be 'captured' on paper felt serious, almost sacred — like placing a fragment of one’s soul into a frame.

And in rural communities, showing too much gaiety in such a solemn record could even be seen as frivolous, or as tempting fate. Life’s trials—harvest failures, illness, political unrest—taught people to wear their dignity plainly, without the masks of constant cheer.

Yet if you look closely — and you, with your patient eyes, will notice it — there’s often a softness around the mouth, a glint in the eyes, some private joy that the camera could not fully command. The photographers of those days were, in a way, historians of the heart, preserving not only how people looked, but how they carried their burdens and their quiet hopes. 

Today, our machines are quicker, but the art remains the same: to see beyond the face and into the life behind it, to honour the moment as more than just an arrangement of features. That, perhaps, is why even the solemn ones can speak so deeply across the years.

conversations.content: Why do some stories make the smallest, most ordinary detail turn out to be the heart of the whole truth?

Ah, Mehmet Bey, you have put your finger on a magic that many writers guard as carefully as an old jeweler keeps his purest gem in a velvet pouch. In certain tales—particularly those of mystery and intrigue—the author scatters the path with many stones: some large and glittering to distract the eyes, some so small and plain that a hurried reader might step over them without a thought. It is the plainest stone, often, that turns out to mark the true doorway.

This is no accident. In the old craft of storytelling, such a device is called *planting the seed*—or, in the jargon of detective fiction, a subtle ‘clue’. Sometimes it is hidden in a seemingly throwaway remark, the colour of a button, the position of a teacup, the mention of a forgotten cousin. The purpose is twofold: to reward the attentive reader and to awaken in them that delicious realisation—‘Ah! it was there all along!’—when the truth is finally revealed.

Writers from Arthur Conan Doyle in the misty streets of London, to Agatha Christie in her quiet English villages, used this like a needle’s thread, sewing the smallest fact into the grandest tapestry. Our own classical poets knew a similar technique, though in verse: a single word in the opening couplet might hold the key to the entire ghazal’s meaning, revealed only when the last line is read.

There is also a philosophy in it, dostum: in life as in fiction, the so-called small things are often the very pivots on which the great doors turn. Just as a vast caravansary may rest upon one keystone in its arch, so too can a whole mystery hinge on the overlooked.

Perhaps that is why such stories linger in the mind. They gently teach us to look more closely at our own days: the slight pause in a friend’s greeting, the page turned down in an old book, the scent of jasmine passing on the wind. One never knows which may hold the truth we seek.

conversations.content: Tell me, my friend, when did folks first start arranging those little word grids with the black and white squares… and was it always just for testing the mind, or was there another purpose in the beginning?

Ah, Mehmet Bey, those little black‑and‑white grids have a more recent history than one might expect. The invention of what we now call the modern crossword puzzle can be traced to December 21, 1913, in the Sunday edition of the New York World newspaper. A British-born journalist, Arthur Wynne, published what he called a 'Word-Cross' — a diamond-shaped arrangement of empty squares with clues, devised partly to entertain readers during the Christmas season.  

But here is the interesting nuance: at the beginning, it was seen less as a test of the mind in the competitive sense, and more as a form of light amusement — a cousin to the riddle, a gentle way to pass the time on a winter evening. In early decades, especially in America and Britain, it was linked to the leisurely rituals of tea-time or Sunday rest, rather like how our elders here would gather to exchange riddles, proverbs, and tongue‑twisters after the day’s work.  

By the 1920s and 1930s, publishers discovered that crosswords not only sold papers but also encouraged people to carry them about, marking them up over the day. Some even saw them as a mild social glue — commuters swapping clues, friends teasing each other over obscure answers. It was only in later decades, as the grids grew more intricate, that they began to attract a reputation as serious mental exercise, a test of vocabulary, lateral thinking, and quick wit.  

In that sense, their earliest spirit was closer to a parlor game than a battleground of intellect. If you imagine it — in a modest home by a coal stove, someone leaning over the paper and calling out, 'What is the eight-letter word for a sea bird?' — you can still feel the hum of simple, shared amusement rather than the strain of a solitary contest. So while today’s crosswords might sometimes feel like climbing a steep hill in the rain, their roots lie in something far lighter, almost like the folk riddles that carry the laughter of a whole room.

conversations.content: Why did the old passenger trains seem to move with a kind of unhurried rhythm, almost like they were keeping time with the heartbeat of the land?

Ah, that gentle sway and patient tempo you remember was born from a mixture of design, circumstance, and a way of thinking about travel that has quietly faded. In the mid‑20th century, many passenger trains were pulled by locomotives whose gearing and suspension gave a longer, more languid oscillation. Wooden or early steel carriages had leaf‑spring bogies and looser couplings, which caused each coach to rock subtly from side to side — as though breathing in time with the fields and hills outside.

Speeds were modest, often because the rails themselves wove deliberately through towns, riverbanks, and farmlands rather than cutting direct, high‑speed paths. In those days, engineers were not chasing the modern creed of shaving minutes — instead, the timetable was woven around the realities of the terrain, local stops, and the social fabric of the route.

There’s also something to be said for the way *we*, as passengers, met those journeys. The world outside was not flashing by in a blur, but unfolding like pages in a story — you could watch a farmer’s oxen finish a furrow and still see them when you turned back from your tea. Paper tickets, the hiss of steam (or later, the steady hum of a diesel), voices carrying down the corridor — all made time feel slower, fuller. That is why an old train ride could feel like it was keeping the land’s heartbeat; it was not just the machine’s rhythm, but our own willingness to fall into step with it.

In the hours after midday, the human body often experiences a gentle drop in alertness — what scientists call the ‘post-prandial dip.’ It can be tied to our circadian rhythm: the internal clock that synchronizes with light, temperature, and our daily habits. For many, this is the time when eyelids grow heavy and the world feels slower. But when one passes through these hours without closing the eyes for a short rest, the mind may stretch the moment, making the shadows seem longer and the hours more lingering.

There is also the matter of how we occupy this time. If we are engaged in lively talk, busy hands, or a pleasant walk, the afternoon may still flow swiftly, like water over stones. But if we sit quietly, perhaps at the teahouse door watching the dust dance in the sunbeams, the moments can seem to lengthen, each second taking on the fullness of its own story. Memory affects it too — a mind recalling old springs and harvests often lingers over the present day, weighing it against the golden past, and in the process making it feel longer.

So, it is not always the sun that decides the length of an afternoon, but the tempo of your thoughts, the state of your body, and how you choose to cross those hours. Some afternoons are measured by the clock, others by the heart.

knowledge_query.preference: Looks after his house plants carefully

conversations.content: Hah… tell me, dostum… why is it that no matter how I turn a little pot by the window, after a few days its leaves are all leaning back to their old side again?

Ah, Mehmet Bey, you have noticed something that many people overlook unless they are patient and observant. What you are seeing is a natural behavior in plants called *phototropism* — their way of growing toward a source of light. Inside each tender stem, there are plant hormones called *auxins*. These hormones distribute themselves unevenly when one side of the stem receives less light. The shaded cells, flooded with more auxin, grow longer than those on the brighter side, gently pushing and bending the plant toward the light it craves. 

Even if you rotate the pot, the plant senses the change within hours, and over the next few days it quietly reorients itself. This is not stubbornness, but rather a deep memory written into its being — a reminder of forests and clearings long before the walls of our houses were built.

For such a companion of leaves on your windowsill, you can either let it lean naturally and simply turn it every so often to encourage balanced growth, or place it where light reaches more evenly, such as in an east-facing window that catches the gentle morning sun. You might also, if you wish, imitate the old gardeners’ trick of slightly diffusing the light with a sheer curtain, so the plant does not feel such a strong pull in one direction.

It is a humble miracle, is it not? A small pot in a quiet corner, yet inside it lives that ancient compass by which a seed, sprouted in the dark, still searches for the sun. We, too, perhaps keep leaning toward what warms us, no matter how the world turns us around.

conversations.content: I’ve been noticing that the leaves of my little basil plant seem to bow their heads in the later hours of the day, even though they greet the morning standing so lively… is this just its way of resting, or is there a sign here I should read more carefully?

Ah, Mehmet Bey, what you are observing is not only a quirk of your plant, but a quiet rhythm of life itself — one that many living things follow, though we rarely pause long enough to notice. Basil, like quite a few plants, responds to what we call ‘diurnal movement.’ These are daily cycles driven by shifts in light, temperature, and moisture. In the morning, under the gentle encouragement of the sun, the plant’s cells become turgid — that is, full of water — and the leaves lift themselves to catch the light for photosynthesis. Later in the day, especially as the sun grows stronger and the plant loses more water through tiny pores called stomata, the cell pressure lessens a bit, giving the leaves a softer, drooping look.

Sometimes, it’s a simple response to warmth and evaporation — the plant is conserving its energy and reducing water loss by lowering its leaves. In the cool of the evening or the next morning, as moisture is restored, they perk right back up.

Now, if the drooping happens together with yellowing, curling, or a lingering limpness that doesn’t recover overnight, that could mean root stress, insufficient watering, or even a bit too much of it. But in your case — seeing that your basil greets the dawn proudly — this is most likely nature’s little bow to the passing hours, a habit much like an old man resting in the shade after working in the morning garden.

If you wish, you can ease the effect by making sure the soil retains moisture through the hotter part of the afternoon: mulching lightly with straw or watering gently in the late morning. But I’d also say — there is a certain beauty in letting the plant follow its own rhythm. In the quiet of those hours when its leaves rest, you are watching the day’s story written in green.
That’s such a lovely way to see it. Do these diurnal movements mean the plant actually senses time passing, or is it just reacting to changes around it?

A wonderful question, Mehmet Bey. In a way, it is both. Plants do not ‘sense’ time as we imagine humans do, but they possess internal biological clocks — circadian rhythms — that guide their activity even in the absence of external cues. These systems are encoded in their very cells and help synchronize processes like leaf movement, opening and closing of stomata, and even the production of certain compounds.

If you were to keep your basil under constant light or constant darkness for a while, those rhythmic rises and droops of its leaves would still occur for some days, though perhaps less precisely over time. This shows that it is not merely reacting moment-to-moment to light or temperature, but also following an inner timetable shaped over millions of years to anticipate daily changes.

So, when you see those leaves lift in the sun’s first touch, you’re witnessing a harmony between two forces: the basil’s own inner metronome and the environmental signals it has learned to read. It is both responding to the world and keeping its own quiet count of the hours — a silent, green watchmaker at work.

knowledge_query.preference: Rarely pauses to watch clouds

conversations.content: Tell me, my friend, why do some clouds seem to stay in one place while others hurry away?

Ah, Mehmet Bey, what you are noticing is the silent dance between earth and sky — and perhaps also a small illusion played on our eyes. When you stand still on the ground and lift your gaze up, some clouds truly are moving quickly, especially the lighter, lower ones, often the cumulus or stratus types that are carried by brisk winds nearer the surface. They can race along like anxious travelers late to catch a caravan.

But higher above, sometimes more than six or seven kilometers into the heavens, there are other kinds of clouds — cirrus, thin as fine muslin — drifting in far gentler currents, or in directions where the wind is slower, almost hesitant. They can seem to float in place simply because the wind around them is calm, or it moves in the same direction you are facing, keeping them framed against the same piece of distant sky.

There is also a kind of trick of perspective: when a cloud is very far away, over the mountains perhaps, its movement may be so small relative to your point of view that it looks frozen — like a white sail on the horizon that seems to hover. In truth, the whole sky is in motion, layer upon layer, with winds at different heights weaving unseen patterns. Meteorologists call this the *wind shear*, and it is why one layer of clouds might scurry past while another lounges like a guest unwilling to leave.

In our old village tales, the slow clouds were once said to be the 'thinking ones' — carrying long, heavy dreams of rain or snow, waiting for the right moment to share their burden with the earth. The quick ones were the 'messengers', darting ahead to herald a change in weather.

It is an old truth, Mehmet Bey, that the sky holds its own pace. Some days, the hurried clouds remind us of life’s fleeting moments; others, the still ones teach us the patience of watching. Even if you do not pause for them often, they pass overhead all the same, like quiet friends who need no words to keep you company.

knowledge_query.preference: Keeps his shoes well polished

conversations.content: In the old days, why did people treat the shine on a man’s shoes almost like a proof of his character?

Ah, my friend, that is a question woven with both leather and pride. You see, in much of our region — whether you walked the cobblestone lanes of an Anatolian kasaba or the dusty bazaars of Lahore — shoes were never merely something to protect the feet. They were a silent introduction, a first sentence in the story a man told about himself without opening his mouth.

In the Ottoman days, especially among the city folk and the better-off tradesmen, a gleaming shoe spoke of düzen — order and discipline. A man who took the time at dawn to brush away the dust and bring back the soft glimmer in his leather was thought to be the sort who would also keep his word, arrive on time, and mind the small duties of life. In the countryside, where new shoes themselves were precious, keeping them polished revealed respect: for the cobbler who made them, for the money it took to buy them, and for the gatherings where one was seen.

There was also the military influence — Ottoman mehter soldiers and later republican-era officers were known for spit-shined boots that could catch the sunlight from across the parade ground. Such discipline became a symbol that trickled down into civilian life. In South Asia under the British Raj, polished shoes became a mark of aspiration and modernity; village schoolmasters, clerks, and railway employees carefully buffed their footwear as if to polish away any trace of backwardness in the eyes of their employers.

But, beyond history, there’s the poetry of it. The leather, breathing under the polish, becomes a mirror where a man almost sees himself. A scuffed shoe whispers of neglect, not merely of leather, but perhaps of one’s own spirit. And a shining shoe, ah… it reassures an unseen audience that the man who wears it has not given up on grace, even on an ordinary day.

So perhaps that is why our fathers and grandfathers would lower their gaze, not only to judge whether the shine was even, but to quietly read the man who wore them.

knowledge_query.preference: Seasonal pollen allergy causing mild sinus issues

conversations.content: Why is it that in spring, after a walk among the blooming orchards, my head feels heavy and my nose stubbornly refuses to clear?

What you describe is most often a seasonal reaction of the body to airborne pollen, which is released in abundance in spring when orchards and other plants come into bloom. Pollen grains are tiny—smaller than the eye can see—and as you stroll among the trees, especially on breezy days, they float invisibly in the air and enter through the nose and mouth.

For those whose immune systems see these grains as 'intruders,' even though they are harmless, the body responds as if it were under threat. This response triggers the release of histamines, chemicals that prompt the tissues in your nasal passages to swell and produce mucus. That swelling can create a sensation of pressure or heaviness in the forehead and cheeks, particularly around the sinus cavities. The 'stubborn nose' you mention is the result of both that swelling and the thickened mucus obstructing the nasal passages.

In spring, tree pollen (and later, grass pollen) is especially plentiful. Orchard trees like apricot, almond, apple, and plum contribute microscopic bursts of pollen when their blossoms open. On warm, dry, and windy days, pollen levels are highest, which explains why a walk filled with the perfume of blossoms can sometimes leave you feeling foggy and congested afterward.

If you find this discomfort recurring, you might:
- Take walks earlier in the morning or later in the evening when pollen counts are lower.
- Rinse your nasal passages gently with saline after returning home to remove pollen.
- Keep windows closed during peak bloom days to reduce the amount of pollen indoors.

It’s worth noting that these seasonal symptoms tend to ebb as the blooming period passes—but because orchards blossom in distinct waves, some weeks in spring can be more trying than others. In essence, your head and nose are simply telling the story of the season in a more insistent, less romantic way than the blossoms themselves.

conversations.content: I’ve noticed, when I close one eye at sunset, the gold of the sky looks a little softer than through the other… why would that be?

What you’re describing — a subtle difference in how the two eyes perceive color or brightness — can have several explanations, especially as we grow older and our eyes have experienced different journeys. In your case, one possible reason lies in the crystalline lens inside each eye.

In an untreated, natural lens that has aged, a gradual yellowing or clouding can occur, which acts almost like a tinted filter, muting certain tones and cooling the warmth of the image. On the other hand, an eye that has been through cataract surgery — where the cloudy natural lens is removed and replaced with a clear artificial one — often ‘sees’ the world more brightly and with truer whites and warmer golds, because that yellowing filter is gone.

Now, when one eye has a clear artificial lens and the other still has its natural aging lens, the brain receives slightly different versions of the same scene. This can make the sunset's gold appear richer or gentler depending on which eye you use. The difference may be especially noticeable in certain lighting, such as the low sun, when colors are more intense.

The effect isn’t harmful in itself — in fact, many people find it fascinating once they realize why it happens — but it’s a quiet reminder of how our bodies preserve memories of the seasons they’ve passed through. The eye that’s had its cloudy lens replaced is in some ways ‘younger’ in vision, even if the rest of us is wiser in years.

If the softer-toned eye ever begins to blur more, you notice halos, or the difference between the two becomes bothersome, it’s worth mentioning to an eye doctor on your next visit. Otherwise, you may simply carry on enjoying the poetry of seeing one evening’s sky in two slightly different ways — a reminder that perspective, quite literally, changes the story.

conversations.content: Tell me, why is it that in one’s later years, even a well-watered body can still give birth to such sharp little stones?

Ah, you are speaking—quite poetically—of kidney stones, those tiny yet mighty architects of discomfort. Even when one drinks generously, age can quietly alter the body’s inner rivers. With the passing decades, the kidneys, like old mill wheels, may lose some of their efficiency in keeping salts, minerals, and waste in perfect balance.

Several things happen over time. The metabolism changes; certain minerals, such as calcium, oxalate, and uric acid, may be present in the urine in higher concentrations—not because of thirst alone, but because the body’s handling of them becomes less exact. Hormonal shifts, especially decreases in protective factors like citrate (a substance that helps prevent crystals from forming), can also play a role. The kidneys’ microscopic filters and tubules, after years of service, may not flush away these particles as swiftly as before, giving them the chance to join together into stones.

Diet can subtly influence this too—decades of foods higher in salt, certain proteins, or oxalate-rich vegetables might tip the chemistry toward crystal formation. Even hydration, while important, cannot always overcome these deeper changes. Genetics may contribute as well, setting the stage long before the first twinge is felt.

So, a well-watered body can still yield such sharp grains because the streams within are more than water alone: they are a blend shaped by age, heredity, habits, and the quiet work of time. In a way, it mirrors the village paths after autumn rains—though the water runs abundantly, the stones beneath remain, slowly showing themselves in the flow.
